schools.summary: Gill weighs the mutual silence — Naomi's modesty in not applying to her wealthy kinsman, and Boaz's ignorance of her want.
Henry reads the rich kinsman and the poor widow as providence's deliberate arrangement, teaching the great to own their poor relations.
Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott treat the notice as narrative groundwork and trace Boaz's genealogy through Salmon and Rahab.
Clarke notes Boaz's substance and the tradition that he was one of Israel's judges.
The gleaning laws secured the poor's right, yet hard-hearted owners often obstructed it — hence Ruth's hope to find grace in someone's sight.
Clarke and Wesley stress Ruth's modesty in asking as a favour what the law already gave her as a right.
Gill commends the industrious, virtuous woman unwilling to live an idle life.
Henry makes Ruth a fourfold example of humility, industry, filial regard, and dependence on Providence.
'Her chance chanced' — she knew neither the field's owner nor his kinship, yet her steps were shaped by unseen hands.
A chance in reference to second causes, but ordered by God's providence, who wisely governs the smallest events.
What seemed casual to Ruth was Providence directing her steps for God's glory and his people's good.
Gill relays the Midrash's picture of the stranger carefully marking her unfamiliar way.
Clarke savours the Jehovah-greetings, and Wesley presses piety in civil conversation, 'which now so many are ashamed of.'
Mutual blessing between master and reapers shows the good-will and joint dependence on God that make a household prosper.
Boaz's presence in his own field shows diligence and humility — 'the eye of the master is the most fruitful thing in the field.'
Ellicott sees a true gentleman — supervision without fault-finding, courtesy joined with gravity.
Gill details the overseer's office — directing labour, providing food, paying wages — from Josephus and Jewish tradition.
Clarke parallels the steward with the master of Homer's harvest field on the shield of Achilles.
Henry turns the faithful overseer into a figure of ministers, servants set over God's house.
Keil and Delitzsch see in Boaz's question the first motion of a kindness already purposed toward Naomi's daughter-in-law.
The servant had observed and conversed with Ruth, and so could answer his master truly.
The steward's fair account — stranger, kinswoman, proselyte, modest, industrious — recommended Ruth to his master's charity.
The good report of her modesty and diligence strengthened Boaz's purpose to speak kindly to her.
Wesley: she modestly asked leave, laboured diligently, and rested but a little in the field-booth of those hot countries.
The Hebrew stresses unbroken diligence from the very morning onward, with only a little sitting in the house.
Henry notes she gleaned only after asking leave, and relays the conjecture that her brief retirement was for devotion.
Gill marks these as the overseer's continued words, an eyewitness account of her conduct.
'My daughter' bespeaks fatherly kindness from an older man; 'cleave to my maidens' is courtesy guarding the stranger from rudeness.
Wesley: placed with the maidens, not the young men, to avoid occasion of sin and scandal; Clarke supplies the field-craft of gleaning behind them.
Henry hears in 'abide here fast' the soul's summons to keep to the Redeemer's field.
The charge not to touch her was needful for an unprotected stranger; 'touch' covers any incivility or injury.
Ellicott: the young men 'shall from time to time draw' — perhaps from the very well of Bethlehem David longed for.
Henry sees the good master who ensures his servants do no hurt, and welcomes the stranger to Bethlehem's water.
Gill fits each kindness to her work — eyes on the field, following after the reapers.
Great humility under a deep sense of favour, shown in the utmost civil respect.
Clarke explains the Eastern prostration and relays the Targum's dialogue on Moab's exclusion and Ruth's promised kings and prophets.
Keil and Delitzsch compare Abigail's obeisance and define 'take knowledge' as receiving kindly; Ellicott notes 'stranger' means foreigner outright.
Good breeding adorns religion; she owns herself unworthy and magnifies a small kindness.
Gill relays Alshech: Boaz raised his voice so that all standing by might hear the stranger publicly honoured.
Leaving father, mother, and homeland for an unknown people, Ruth has done what God commanded Abraham to do.
Those that do well ought to have the praise of it; those that leave all for true religion deserve double honour.
The blessing weaves Genesis and Deuteronomy together: reward language from Abraham, the wing-refuge from the eagle of the Song of Moses.
Works are not meritorious, yet God of his own grace recompenses the labour of love.
The wings picture chicks sheltering from hawk and storm — or the Cherubim between which God dwelt; Ruth's conversion is already evident.
Those who come by faith under the wings of divine grace may be sure of a full recompense; the proselyte is 'gathered under the wings.'
Gill reads 'I shall find favour' — the acknowledgment and confidence of faith.
Keil and Delitzsch take the verb as an optative wish, citing Carpzov's paraphrase of her self-abasement; Ellicott finds 'friendly' literally 'to the heart.'
Unworthy as any maidservant, yet shown distinguished kindness; she humbly implores its continuance.
The great know not how much good a kind word does; Boaz's friendly speech was a cordial to her.

debates.question: Is Ruth's 'let me find favour' a confident acknowledgment or a humble petition?

sides.position: an expression of faith and assurance — 'I shall find favour'
an optative wish for continued kindness — 'may I find favour'

schools.summary: Chomets is sour wine with oil, still the reapers' drink, and the parched corn roasted grain; Ellicott links the vinegar to Nazarite law and the cross.
Clarke traces the dipped morsel among the Arabs; Wesley notes she sat apart as an inferior and the fine hand reached forth to the needy.
God allows relishing as well as nourishing food; Boaz scatters yet increases, and Ruth's temperance befriends her industry.
She rose straight from the meal to glean again — industry unrelaxed by favour.
Gleaning among the sheaves was not generally allowed; Boaz's directions went far beyond compassion and show a heart already won.
Clarke: a privilege, for none might glean till the sheaves were bound and the shocks set up.
The servants must not call her thief — her honour guarded as carefully as her portion.
Gill details the contrivance — handfuls scattered where they were reaped instead of laid in order.
Ears drawn from the bundles themselves and left for her, with no scolding allowed.
The greatest kindness to the poor is to assist and encourage their industry; masters should allow their servants to be charitable.
Constant at her mean employment from morning to night.
Be not weary in well-doing; thresh what you glean; many a little makes a great deal; ask at evening, 'Where have I gleaned to-day?'
Clarke computes the ephah at seven gallons and a half — a good day's work.
She threshed to save carrying the straw — some twenty to twenty-five pounds of barley, four pecks and more.
She drew from her pocket, as the Chaldee supplies, what she had left of the parched corn.
The portion reserved at dinner, more than she could eat, was carried home to her mother-in-law.
Industry abroad, piety at home — children maintaining parents is the honour the fifth commandment requires.
Gill pictures her shouldering the whole ephah into the city.
The unusual quantity told Naomi that special kindness had been shown, and her blessing went out before the benefactor's name.
Parents should enquire into their children's ways with mildness; Naomi shoots the arrow of prayer at a venture.
Wesley turns the question inward: a good evening self-examination — where have I gleaned to-day?
Naomi supposed Providence had directed Ruth to a good spot or a liberal hand.
Naomi's hope rekindles at the name: the kind stranger is the goel — and our blessed Saviour is our Goel who has the right to redeem.
Clarke expounds the goel's threefold office — inheritance, blood, and the childless widow; Wesley reads the unbroken kindness as Boaz's.
Ellicott leaves the antecedent open between God and Boaz, and sees Naomi's silence toward her kinsman as independence of character.
Gill hears Naomi invoking every blessing, temporal and spiritual, upon the man.

debates.question: Whose kindness 'hath not left off' — the Lord's or Boaz's?

sides.position: Boaz's, whose old kindness to Elimelech's house now continues to his widow
the antecedent is grammatically open — God or Boaz, either yielding good sense

schools.summary: The masculine 'young men' is the comprehensive gender for the whole reaping household — emphatically 'my people,' to the end of 'my harvest.'
Clarke would translate 'servants,' male and female alike; Wesley notes she repeats the kindness but not the commendation.
Past favours give reason to expect more — a standing invitation for the whole season.
The verb means to fall upon and ill-treat: keeping to Boaz's maidens secured Ruth from insult, and no correction of Ruth is intended.
Another field would invite injury from strangers and slight Boaz's kindness.
Henry's allegory: Christ is our Goel — let us not be found in any other field, nor seek satisfaction in the creature.

debates.question: Does Naomi's mention of the maidens tacitly correct Ruth's 'young men'?

sides.position: yes — a gentle rebuke steering Ruth toward fitter company
holders: some older interpreters reported by Matthew Henry

sides.position: no distinction intended — Ruth named the chief workers, Naomi the natural companions

schools.summary: A wonderful instance of dutiful affection, humility, and industry.
Through both harvests she kept by the maidens; 'and dwelt with her mother-in-law' quietly signals the change about to come.
Working days, not merry nights — Ruth's home-keeping contrasted with Dinah's gadding, her diligence ending in preferment.
Providence wove her modesty and industry into the line of Christ; the path of duty proved the way of safety.
